entries.title: Banach Space
entries.content: A complete normed vector space, where every Cauchy sequence converges to a limit within the space.
entries.example: The space of real numbers R with absolute norm

entries.title: Barycenter
entries.content: The center of mass of two or more bodies that orbit each other, or the centroid of a geometric shape.
entries.example: Barycenter of the Earth-Moon system

entries.title: Base Ten
entries.content: The decimal number system that uses ten distinct digits (0-9) to represent numbers.

entries.title: Bayes' Theorem
entries.content: A formula that describes the probability of an event based on prior knowledge of conditions related to the event.
entries.example: Calculating probability of disease given positive test

entries.title: Bell Curve
entries.content: The informal name for the graph of a normal distribution in statistics.
entries.example: Distribution of heights in a population

entries.title: Bernoulli Trial
entries.content: A random experiment with exactly two possible outcomes: success and failure.
entries.example: Tossing a coin (heads or tails)

entries.title: Bessel Function
entries.content: Canonical solutions of Bessel's differential equation, useful in wave propagation and physics.
entries.example: Solving electromagnetic waves inside a cylinder

entries.title: Beta Distribution
entries.content: A family of continuous probability distributions defined on the interval [0, 1] parameterized by two positive shape parameters.
entries.formula: f(x; alpha, beta) = x^(alpha-1)*(1-x)^(beta-1) / B(alpha, beta)
entries.example: Modeling probabilities or proportions in Bayesian inference

entries.title: Bezout's Identity
entries.content: A theorem in number theory stating that the greatest common divisor of two integers can be expressed as a linear combination of them.

entries.title: Bijective Function
entries.content: A function that is both injective (one-to-one) and surjective (onto), meaning every element maps to a unique element.
entries.example: f(x) = x + 1 is a bijection on real numbers

entries.title: Bilinear Form
entries.content: A bilinear map from V x V to the field of scalars, linear in both arguments.
entries.formula: B(u + v, w) = B(u, w) + B(v, w) etc.
entries.example: Dot product of vectors

entries.title: Bimodal Distribution
entries.content: A probability distribution with two distinct modes or peaks.
entries.formula: Graph shows two local maximum heights
entries.example: Distribution of restaurant customer counts at lunch and dinner

entries.title: Binary Number System
entries.content: A base-2 numeral system that represents numeric values using two unique symbols: 0 and 1.
entries.example: Binary 1010 equals decimal 10

entries.title: Binary Operation
entries.content: A rule that combines two elements of a set to produce another element of the set.
entries.example: Addition on integers

entries.title: Binary Search Tree
entries.content: A node-based binary tree data structure where each node has a key greater than all keys in its left subtree.
entries.example: Searching for a number in O(log n) time

entries.title: Binomial Coefficient
entries.content: The number of ways to choose k elements from a set of n elements without regard to order.

entries.title: Binomial Distribution
entries.content: The discrete probability distribution of the number of successes in a sequence of n independent Bernoulli trials.
entries.example: Flipping a coin 10 times and counting heads

entries.title: Binomial Theorem
entries.content: A formula for expanding powers of binomials algebraically.
entries.example: Expanding (x+y)^2 = x^2 + 2xy + y^2

entries.title: Bipartite Graph
entries.content: A graph whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that no two graph vertices in the same set are connected.
entries.formula: V = V_1 union V_2, E only between V_1 and V_2
entries.example: A matching problem between jobs and applicants

entries.title: Birthday Paradox
entries.content: The counter-intuitive probability that in a group of 23 people, there is a 50% chance two share a birthday.
entries.example: In a room of 30 people, probability of match is ~70%

entries.title: Bisector
entries.content: A line, ray, or segment that cuts another geometric figure (like a line segment or angle) into two equal parts.
entries.example: Perpendicular bisector of a line segment

entries.title: Bivariate Data
entries.content: Data that involves two different variables, which are measured on the same units to analyze relationships.
entries.example: Analyzing study hours vs exam scores

entries.title: Block Matrix
entries.content: A matrix partitioned into smaller matrices called blocks.
entries.example: Subdividing a large matrix for faster computation

entries.title: Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem
entries.content: A fundamental theorem in analysis stating that every bounded sequence in R^n has a convergent subsequence.
entries.example: Selecting convergent terms from sequence (-1)^n * (1 - 1/n)

entries.title: Boolean Algebra
entries.content: A branch of algebra where variable values are truth values: true (1) and false (0).
entries.formula: x AND y; x OR y; NOT x
entries.example: Logic gate operations in microchips

entries.title: Boolean Function
entries.content: A function that maps boolean inputs (0 or 1) to a boolean output.
entries.example: f(x, y) = x AND NOT y

entries.title: Boolean Matrix
entries.content: A matrix whose entries are elements of a Boolean algebra, typically 0 and 1.
entries.example: Representing relations between sets

entries.title: Boundary Value Problem
entries.content: A differential equation together with a set of additional constraints called boundary conditions.
entries.example: Heat distribution in a wire with fixed end temperatures

entries.title: Bounded Function
entries.content: A function whose values are all contained within a fixed interval of real numbers.
entries.example: f(x) = sin(x) is bounded because |sin(x)| <= 1

entries.title: Bounded Sequence
entries.content: A sequence of numbers whose values never exceed a certain upper bound or fall below a lower bound.
entries.formula: m <= a_n <= M for all n
entries.example: Sequence 1/n is bounded between 0 and 1

entries.title: Box-and-Whisker Plot
entries.content: A graphical display summarizing a data set using five summaries: minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum.
entries.example: Plotting test scores to see dispersion

entries.title: Boxplot Median
entries.content: The central horizontal line inside the box of a boxplot, representing the 50th percentile of the dataset.
entries.example: Finding median score in a boxplot

entries.title: Brahmagupta's Formula
entries.content: A formula for the area of a cyclic quadrilateral given its four side lengths.
entries.formula: Area = sqrt((s-a)(s-b)(s-c)(s-d)) where s is semiperimeter
entries.example: Quadrilateral inscribed in a circle with sides 3, 4, 5, 6

entries.title: Branch Cut
entries.content: A curve in the complex plane across which a multi-valued analytic function is discontinuous.
entries.example: Defining a single-valued branch of the square root function

entries.title: Breadth-First Search
entries.content: An algorithm for traversing or searching tree or graph structures, starting at the root and exploring neighbor nodes first.
entries.example: Finding the shortest path in an unweighted grid

entries.title: Briggsian Logarithm
entries.content: Another name for the common logarithm, which uses base 10.

entries.title: Buffon's Needle
entries.content: A classic probability problem that estimates the value of pi by dropping needles on parallel lines.
entries.example: Dropping needles of length 2cm on lines 4cm apart

entries.title: Butterfly Effect
entries.content: A phenomenon in chaos theory where tiny differences in initial conditions produce widely differing outcomes.
entries.example: Weather prediction limits

entries.title: Base of Natural Logarithm
entries.content: The mathematical constant e, approximately equal to 2.71828, which is the base of natural logarithms.
entries.example: e^x derivative is e^x

entries.title: Basis of Vector Space
entries.content: A set of linearly independent vectors in a vector space that span the entire space.
entries.example: Vectors (1,0) and (0,1) form a basis for R^2
